summary: Spolsky sets up a common complaint about software bloat by noting that Excel grew from 15MB to 146MB between 1993 and 2000. He frames the tenfold increase as seemingly damning evidence of sloppy programming, then immediately subverts the reader's expectation by linking to an article that argues the opposite. The post is a teaser that challenges the knee-jerk reaction that growing software size equals poor engineering.
key_points: Excel grew from 15MB to 146MB between version 5.0 (1993) and Excel 2000, nearly a tenfold increase
The instinctive reaction is to blame 'sloppy Microsoft programmers' for bloated software
Spolsky deliberately sets up this assumption only to immediately refute it, linking to his longer essay on why software bloat isn't what it seems
money_quotes: It was positively huge: it required a whole 15 megabytes of hard drive space.
In those days we could still remember our first 20MB PC hard drives (around 1985) and so 15MB sure seemed like a lot.
Dang those sloppy Microsoft programmers, right? Wrong.
key_insight: The instinctive assumption that growing software size proves sloppy programming is wrong, and the real story behind software bloat is more nuanced than it appears.
